sound_type: duck-like clucking chorus -- rapid, low-pitched, raspy clucking resembling duck quacking from breeding aggregations in vernal pools
ecosystem: Vernal pool. Temporary seasonal pools that fill with snowmelt and spring rain, dry by summer. Also woodland ponds, marshes, shallow wetlands.
season: Early spring. Among the very first amphibians to breed -- often while snow is still on the ground and ice is still on the pond.

coordinates.description: Reference for vernal pool habitat in the PNW, Washington. Wood frogs breed in temporary woodland pools.

taxonomy_note.description: RANIDAE -- NEW FAMILY (46th). Anura continues. True frogs. Second anuran family after Hylidae (86, 90). Third amphibian entry. FREEZE TOLERANCE -- survives being frozen solid.
taxonomy_note.infraclass_note: Amphibia. Returning to amphibians after the insect era.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Ranidae from Latin rana ('frog'). Lithobates from Greek lithos ('stone') + bates ('one who treads'). Sylvaticus from Latin ('of the woods'). The stone-treading frog of the woods.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Lithobates sylvaticus. One of the most widespread frogs in North America. Northernmost range of any North American frog.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Anura -- continuing frog order. Third anuran entry (86, 90, 96). Second anuran family (Ranidae after Hylidae).
taxonomy_note.class_note: Amphibia -- returning to amphibians after nine insect entries (87-95). The insect era ends. The amphibian thread resumes.
taxonomy_note.family_position: 46th family at degree 96. NEW FAMILY. Ranidae enters. True frogs. First freeze-tolerant species.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Approximately 1,000-2,500 Hz. Lower than Pacific Treefrog and Spring Peeper.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 1,200-2,000 Hz. The duck-like quality from rapid repetition and raspy timbre.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Male advertisement call: rapid series of short clucking notes, approximately 4-8 notes per second.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual call bout: 1-3 seconds. Chorus: continuous during breeding, often 1-2 weeks compressed season.
song_metrics.note_structure: Rapid series of short, raspy clucking notes. Percussive. Duck-like quacking quality.
song_metrics.note_types: Advertisement call (duck-like cluck). Release call (shorter chirp when grasped by another male).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Rapid clucking series. Multiple males calling creates overlapping chorus with duck-like quality.
song_metrics.timbre: Raspy, percussive, guttural. Low-pitched for a frog. Duck-like. Not bell-like (spring peeper) or sharp (Pacific Treefrog). Warm but rough-edged.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Chorus is conspicuous and carries well. Breeding aggregation amplifies. Vernal pool acts as natural amphitheater.
song_metrics.tempo: Rapid clucking: 4-8 notes per second within a call bout.
song_metrics.context: BREEDING CHORUS. Males call from vernal pools in early spring, often while snow is still on the ground. Among the very first amphibian calls of the year. Extremely compressed breeding season (1-2 weeks).
song_metrics.regional_variation: Wood frogs found across northern North America. PNW populations in forest habitats with suitable vernal pools.
song_metrics.key_property: DUCK-LIKE CLUCKING CHORUS -- the 97th acoustic element (species side). Rapid, low-pitched, raspy clucking from vernal pool breeding aggregations. E=4. NEW FAMILY Ranidae (46th). FREEZE TOLERANCE -- survives being frozen solid. Vernal pool habitat (first temporary habitat). Thirty-sixth consecutive original CSV.

physical_description.overall: Small to medium frog, 35-75 mm (1.4-3.0 inches) body length. Tan, brown, or rust-colored. Distinctive dark 'robber's mask' across eyes.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 96: (1) DARK MASK across each eye -- the 'robber's mask,' the most distinctive field mark. (2) LIGHT DORSOLATERAL RIDGES -- raised folds running from eye to hip. (3) TAN-BROWN COLORING -- woodland camouflage. (4) RELATIVELY SMALL -- compact, athletic body.
physical_description.skin_detail: Smooth to slightly granular skin. Tan, brown, or rust-colored. Dark brown or black 'mask' from tympanum through eye. White or cream belly. Dorsolateral ridges prominent.
physical_description.dental_detail: Maxillary teeth present (tiny, used to grip prey). Tongue projectile for catching invertebrates.
physical_description.field_id: Small brown frog with distinctive dark mask across eyes. Found near vernal pools and woodland ponds, especially in early spring. The duck-like clucking chorus is diagnostic -- often mistaken for ducks. Extremely early spring breeder.

nodes.title: Duck-Like Clucking Chorus -- 97th Acoustic Element (species side)
nodes.description: Rapid, low-pitched, raspy clucking from vernal pool breeding aggregations. E=4. Resembles duck quacking. Among the earliest amphibian sounds of spring.

nodes.title: Ranidae Enters -- 46th Family (NEW)
nodes.description: First true frogs. Second anuran family (after Hylidae at 86, 90). Third amphibian entry. Anura continues. 46th family in engine.

nodes.title: Freeze Tolerance -- Survives Being Frozen Solid
nodes.description: First freeze-tolerant species. Up to 65% body water as ice. Heart stops. Cryoprotectant glucose at 100x normal. Thaws in spring and resumes activity. Multiple freeze-thaw cycles per winter.

nodes.title: Vernal Pool -- First Temporary Habitat
nodes.description: Temporary seasonal pools. Fill with snowmelt, dry by summer. Fishless -- safe for amphibian larvae. First explicitly temporary habitat in the engine. Woodland setting.

nodes.title: Anuran Family Pair -- Hylidae (86) and Ranidae (96)
nodes.description: Two anuran families ten degrees apart. Pacific Treefrog (Hylidae, E=4) and Wood Frog (Ranidae, E=4). Same energy, different calls, different families. Krek-krek vs duck-like clucking.

nodes.title: The STRFKR Contrast -- Synth-Pop Pulse vs Duck-Like Clucking
nodes.description: Degree 96 pairs wood frog with STRFKR in Part A. Synth-pop pulse (E=5, relentless joyful groove) vs duck-like clucking (E=4, early spring breeding chorus). Both about persistence -- the frog survives the freeze, the band maintains the pulse.

cross_references.relationship: ANURAN FAMILY PAIR! Both frogs (Anura). Pacific Treefrog (86, Hylidae) and Wood Frog (96, Ranidae) -- two anuran families. Both breed in water. Both E=4. Different calls: krek-krek vs duck-like clucking. Ten degrees apart.
AMPHIBIAN TRIO. Three amphibian entries (86, 90, 96). Spring Peeper (90) is high bell-like peep; Wood Frog (96) is low duck-like cluck. Both early spring breeders. Opposite acoustic registers.

WATER CONNECTION. Back-to-back water-associated species. Mayfly (95) in streams/lakes, mass wing buzz E=3; Wood Frog (96) in vernal pools, duck-like clucking E=4. Passive emergence sound vs active vocalization.
AQUATIC SEQUENCE. Three consecutive water-associated species (94, 95, 96). Stonefly in cold streams, mayfly in streams/lakes, wood frog in vernal pools. Three different aquatic habitats.
WOOD-AND-WATER NAMESAKE. Both 'wood' species. Both woodland water habitats. Wood Duck (58) on ponds; Wood Frog (96) in vernal pools. Duck-like connection: Wood Frog's call sounds like a duck.
